macro_segments.title: When the 14th of Nisan Falls on Shabbat: Destruction of Chametz and Terumah

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: The complexity of observing the 14th on Shabbat
Rabbi Meir's position: Destroy all chametz before Shabbat
The Chachamim's position: Destroy chametz on the proper time (14th)
Rabbi Eliezer bar Tzadok: Differentiation between terumah and chulin
The case of Rabbi Eliezer bar Tzadok's father in Yavneh: Supporting evidence for chulin on Shabbat

macro_segments.title: The Person Going to Perform a Mitzvah: Discovering Forgotten Chametz

micro_segments.title: The scenario: Multiple mitzvot on Erev Pesach and the problem of forgotten chametz
First introduction of bittul chametz in the Mishnah: A last resort option
The halachic principle: Osek b'mitzvah patur min hamitzvah versus the lav of owning chametz
Life-threatening danger: When bittul is immediate without calculation
Optional matters (reshut): The obligation to return and destroy chametz

macro_segments.title: The Person Who Left Jerusalem with Consecrated Meat: Geographic Limits of Obligation

micro_segments.title: The case: Leaving Jerusalem with unspoiled sanctified meat beyond the permitted time
The Tzofim boundary: Determining when one may destroy the meat locally
Tosafot's interpretation: Line of sight to Jerusalem as the relevant boundary
Size thresholds for returning: Beitza versus Zayit in different contexts

macro_segments.title: Marriage and Social Hierarchy: Appropriate and Inappropriate Matches

micro_segments.title: The problematic meal of betrothal: First meal versus second meal (sivlonot)
Rav Chisda's clarification: First meal is universally obligatory; debate is about the second
The general principle: A talmid chacham should not benefit from meals that are not for a mitzvah
Examples of inappropriate marriages: Bat kohen with Yisrael; bat talmid chacham with am ha'aretz
Talmidei chachamim marrying daughters of kohanim: Positive examples and outcomes
The obligation to marry within one's class: Hierarchy of daughters to marry off

macro_segments.title: The Stark Language About Am Ha'aretz: Violent Rhetoric and Practical Restrictions

micro_segments.title: Initial characterization: Am ha'aretz as sheketz and their wives as sheretz
Extreme statements: Stabbing am ha'aretz on Yom Kippur and tearing like a fish
Rabbi Akiva's testimony: His former violent feelings toward talmidei chachamim
Characterizations of am ha'aretz behavior: Marrying daughters as abandonment
Mutual hatred and the role of their wives in perpetuating hostility
Practical restrictions on dealings with am ha'aretz: Testimony, trust, and lost objects
Tosafot's nuance: Different categories of am ha'aretz and historical context
